next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Blue Hour after close smelled of boiled lint, cautery gel, and the sweet old leak from the steam line Jo kept meaning to bully the landlord into fixing. Mira had wiped the wetbench twice and still there was pink under one fingernail from a courier's borrowed ear. Tavi had the anomaly wafer under the reader lamp and was lining stripped screws along the lip of the table from smallest to largest, one of his worse habits when he was scared enough to look busy.

Lin sat on an upturned detergent bucket with the intake cards stacked on their knee, reading every label even after they already knew what the bottle or bin was for, as if printed words were another kind of handhold. Jo's old green cardigan swallowed their wrists. A thread had come loose at the cuff and Lin kept winding it around one finger without noticing.

The wafer was not much to look at. A translucent sliver the size of two postage stamps, archive-sealed, one corner chipped when Mira had pried it loose in the records room with her thumbnail and a prayer she had not believed in. She had taped it inside the lining of her bag for two weeks and still checked every morning to make sure it was there. Tonight Tavi had fitted an adapter from a retired wash console to a clinic reader that still thought it was processing skin-tax invoices.

"If this shorts the board, that's on you," Mira said.

Tavi did not look up. "You're the one who stole corporate memory candy."

"You told me to steal proof."

"I told you to steal proof you could use without burning my only decent reader." He gave the cable a small twist and...
open_hooks_to_carry: Let humor come from bodily absurdity, queue logic, and social embarrassment rather than authorial wink.
Show Lin discovering they are good with frightened people.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...t he had told the truth and could not unsay it.

She was still furious. Still hot from him. Still aware of the exact places his hands had been and the exact shape of the confession sitting between them like contaminated equipment nobody could throw out because they still needed it.

That was the worst part: not that the betrayal erased the wanting, but that it did not. The wanting stayed. It had to live now in the same body as the knowledge.

Tavi stood as if to come toward her, saw her face, and stopped himself.

Mira slung her bag over one shoulder. "Tomorrow at eight," she said. "Mrs. Alvarado's knee."

Work. Not pardon. Not dismissal. The only sentence she trusted not to lie.

"Yeah," he said. His voice caught and corrected. "Yeah. Eight."

She pushed through the curtain into the darkened laundromat. The machines were all dead now. Without them the room felt too large, the rows of glass doors like blank eyes. Jo was at the front gate with the padlock in one hand and her egg timer in the other, as if time itself might try to cheat her if she looked away.

"You all right?" Jo asked.

Mira almost gave the easy answer. Instead she said, "No."

Jo considered that, then nodded once as if it were a receipt she could file for later. "Good. Means you noticed something."

Outside, the canal wind had teeth in it. Mira stepped into it with Tavi's taste still in her mouth and the clean ugly fact of him lodged under her ribs: the man who had just held her like she was breakable had once helped CommonFrame calculate how much breakage a bargain body could carry and still turn a profit.
state_deltas_required: Word-of-mouth success brings more clients, stranger carryovers, and enough foot traffic that secrecy starts to fail under its own popularity.
Blue Hour becomes a visible neighborhood institution, which makes future enforcement and public consequence inevitable.
